title: SEGMENT 3 — The five differences and the five drug targets

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The five differences and the five drug targets
Let me make that concrete, because it organizes the whole lecture.
There are really only a handful of major differences between a bacterial cell and one of your cells, and essentially every antibiotic class we have exploits one of them.
If you learn these five, you have the skeleton onto which we'll hang every drug today.
Difference number one, and historically the most important: the cell wall.
Bacteria are wrapped in a rigid mesh called peptidoglycan.
Your cells have no cell wall at all — you have a flexible lipid membrane and nothing rigid outside it.
This is a gift.
Anything that interferes with building peptidoglycan attacks a structure the human body simply does not possess.
So cell-wall-active drugs tend to be among our safest.
This is target category one.
Difference number two: the ribosome.
Both bacteria and humans make protein on ribosomes, so at first glance this looks useless — same machine, right?
But it's not the same machine.
The bacterial ribosome is what we call a 70S ribosome, built from a 30S small subunit and a 50S large subunit.
Your ribosomes, in the cytoplasm, are 80S, built from 40S and 60S subunits.
Different sizes, different RNA sequences, different proteins.
Different enough that we can design drugs that jam the bacterial 70S ribosome and largely leave your 80S ribosome alone.
I say largely — hold that thought, because there's a complication we'll come back to involving your mitochondria.
This is target category two.
Difference number three: bacterial enzymes for copying and reading DNA.
Bacteria use enzymes called DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV to manage the twisting of their DNA, and these particular enzymes don't exist in your cells in that form.
Bacteria also have their own RNA polymerase, distinct from yours.
Attack those and again you've found something selective.
Target category three.
Difference number four: folate metabolism.
This one is subtle and beautiful.
Both bacteria and humans need folate — it's an essential cofactor for making the building blocks of DNA.
But here's the difference: you can't make folate.
You get it from your diet; it's a vitamin for us.
Bacteria, most of them, can't take up folate from the environment — they have to synthesize it from scratch.
So bacteria carry an entire biochemical assembly line for making folate that your cells don't have, because you don't need it.
Block that assembly line and you starve the bacterium of folate while leaving you untouched, because you got your folate from your salad.
Target category four.
Difference number five, and this is the smallest category: the cell membrane itself.
Now, both bacteria and humans have lipid membranes, so this is risky territory — it's the difference that's least different.
But bacterial membranes have a distinct lipid composition, and there are a couple of drugs that exploit that.
Because the difference is so slim, these drugs tend to be our more toxic, last-resort agents.
Target category five.
So — five differences, five target categories.
Cell wall.
Ribosome.
Nucleic acids.
Folate.
Membrane.

Write those down the side of your page, because for the rest of this lecture, every single drug is going to slot into one of those five boxes, and the toxicity of each drug is going to track how big or small the underlying difference is.
Cell wall: huge difference, very safe drugs.
Membrane: tiny difference, toxic drugs.
That pattern will hold up remarkably well.
